questions.question: What three Linux primitives combine to create a container?
options: chroot, signals, pipes
namespaces, cgroups, seccomp
fork, exec, wait
virtual memory, page tables, TLB
questions.explanation: Containers are a composition of three Linux kernel features: namespaces (isolation of what a process can see — PIDs, mounts, network, etc.), cgroups (limits on how much CPU, memory, I/O a process can use), and seccomp (filtering which system calls a process can make).

questions.question: In a new PID namespace, what PID does the first process see itself as?
options: The same PID as the host sees it
PID 0
PID 1 (it becomes the init process of that namespace)
PID -1 (undefined)
questions.explanation: The first process created in a new PID namespace becomes PID 1 — the init process for that namespace. It cannot see processes outside the namespace, and when it exits, all processes in that namespace are killed (just like init on a real system).

questions.question: What happens when a process violates a seccomp filter that uses SECCOMP_RET_KILL?
options: The syscall returns an error (EPERM)
The process is immediately terminated by the kernel
The kernel logs a warning and allows the syscall
The process is paused and sent SIGSTOP
questions.explanation: SECCOMP_RET_KILL causes the kernel to immediately terminate the offending process with SIGSYS (bad system call). This is the most restrictive action — the process gets no chance to handle the error. Other actions include SECCOMP_RET_ERRNO (return an error), SECCOMP_RET_TRAP (send SIGSYS for a handler), and SECCOMP_RET_LOG (allow but log).

questions.question: Why is chroot alone NOT considered a security boundary?
options: chroot only works on ext4 filesystems
A root user can escape chroot via various techniques, and chroot provides no PID, network, or resource isolation
chroot modifies the kernel permanently
chroot requires a reboot to take effect
questions.explanation: chroot only changes the apparent root filesystem. It provides no isolation for PIDs, network, IPC, or users. A root process can escape chroot using techniques like creating a device node and chrooting into it, or using fchdir to get a file descriptor outside the chroot. Real containers use mount namespaces + pivot_root instead.

questions.question: How do Linux containers achieve isolation without a full virtual machine?
options: By running each container on a separate CPU core
Using namespaces to isolate what a process can see and cgroups to limit what resources it can use
By compiling programs differently for each container
By using different operating systems for each container
questions.explanation: Containers use namespaces (PID, network, mount, user, etc.) to give each container its own view of the system — its own PID 1, network stack, and filesystem. Cgroups limit CPU, memory, and I/O. Together they provide isolation without the overhead of running a full guest OS.
